summary_sections.title: Lesson Overview
summary_sections.content_html:
Overview

This lesson covers the fundamental measures of center used to analyze and summarize data sets. You'll learn how to calculate and apply the mean, median, and mode, and understand when each is most appropriate.

summary_sections.title: Definitions and Concepts
summary_sections.content_html:
Key Definitions

What is an elastic collision?

In an elastic collision, both momentum AND kinetic energy are conserved. Objects bounce off each other. The answer is 'One where both momentum and KE are conserved'. The other options — 'One where objects stick together', 'One where momentum is not conserved', and 'Any collision' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

What is a perfectly inelastic collision?

In an inelastic collision, momentum is conserved but kinetic energy is not. In a perfectly inelastic collision, the objects stick together: m₁v₁ + m₂v₂ = (m₁+m₂)v_f. The answer is 'Objects stick together — maximum KE lost'. The other options — 'All energy is conserved', 'Objects bounce apart', and 'No collision occurs' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

What is conserved in ALL types of collisions?

In all collisions, total momentum is conserved (in an isolated system). Elastic collisions also conserve kinetic energy; inelastic do not. The answer is 'Momentum'. The other options — 'Kinetic energy', 'Speed', and 'Force' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.
